exemplar.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained and explicit focus on the prescribed title throughout the essay
Effective linkage of arguments to two areas of knowledge (mathematics and natural sciences)
Coherent and clear arguments supported by well-integrated, concrete examples (e.g., Olympic sprint data, Mercator projection)
Awareness and partial evaluation of contrasting points of view (e.g., Pythagorean theorem vs. statistical models, regional limitations of climate models)
weaknesses: Counterclaims and their implications are unevenly developed (e.g., limited analysis of the Pythagorean theoremâs broader conceptual impact)
Occasional lapses into descriptive or methodological detail rather than sustained critical analysis of knowledge questions
Imprecise use of TOK terminology and informal definitions of key concepts (e.g., âwrongâ vs. âcorrectâ)
Structural and stylistic issues (lack of a clear knowledge question in the introduction, abrupt transitions, inconsistent referencing)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examiner awarded an overall mark of 8 out of 10 for this TOK essay, placing it firmly within the 7â8 band. The essay demonstrates a sustained focus on the prescribed title and effective engagement with two areas of knowledge, using concrete examples to ground clear and coherent arguments. It also shows awareness of alternative perspectives. However, it stops short of the consistently sophisticated evaluation and explicit consideration of implications required for the top band. The exploration of counterclaims is only partially developed, TOK terminology is used imprecisely, and some sections drift into descriptive or methodological detail rather than critical analysis. Structural and referencing weaknesses further limit the essayâs rigour. Balancing its clear strengths against these limitations, the criterion score of 8 reflects the essayâs solid, predominantly effective performance while acknowledging the gaps that prevent a top-band (9â10) award.

annotations.comment: Phrases like âhelpful to advise individualsâ are vague. Replace general language with precise TOK terminology (e.g. predictive reliability, epistemic limitations) to enhance rigour.
The essay does not consider non-Euclidean geometries where the Pythagorean relation fails, missing an opportunity to evaluate the theoremâs domain of applicability.
Grounding the discussion in the studentâs IA on Olympic 100 m sprint data demonstrates effective use of a concrete, personal example to illustrate how linear and non-linear models operate.
The student precisely restates the prescribed title using the exact quotation, establishing clear engagement with the core TOK question from the outset.

The introduction summarizes Boxâs claim descriptively but lacks an explicit knowledge question or thesis statement. To improve coherence, the student should frame a clear knowledge question and outline the analytical path for mathematics and natural sciences.
Inclusion of a data table or graph illustrating model fit would substantiate claims about accuracy versus simplicity and make the evaluation more rigorous.
The student should link the limitations of statistical models explicitly to their broader societal implications (e.g. policy decisions), deepening the discussion of usefulness.
While noting the theoremâs exactness, the student does not explore its implications for Boxâs claim beyond the specific context of right-angled triangles; a deeper theorisation is needed.

The terms âwrongâ and âcorrectâ are used informally without reference to TOK concepts of bias, simplification or model validity. Defining these terms in relation to knowledge frameworks would sharpen the analysis.
The Mercator projection example effectively demonstrates the trade-off between preserving direction and distorting area, illustrating Boxâs notion of useful yet flawed models.
The comparison of linear versus non-linear models remains largely descriptive. The student should critically evaluate how each modelâs assumptions affect real-world usefulness and reliability.

The student identifies environmental systems and geography as areas of knowledge but does not define their scope or justify why ESS and geography are suited for examining model limitations. Clarifying these choices will strengthen focus.
Overfitting is mentioned but without exploring its epistemic consequences. The student could discuss how overfitting undermines predictive power and impacts decision-making based on such models.
Identifying the Pythagorean theorem as an âexactâ model provides a strong counterclaim that challenges Boxâs generalisation about models being inherently wrong.
The formula appears inline as text; in TOK essays equations should be formatted clearly using LaTeX, e.g. $$a^2 + b^2 = c^2$$, to distinguish mathematical claims.

Detailed discussion of the studentâs IA drifts into methodology rather than focusing on knowledge issues. In TOK, personal examples should be tied explicitly to questions about how we know through models.
The section cites ESS and geography but does not explain how these areas define their methods or knowledge criteria; clarifying the methodology of climate modelling would be beneficial.
To enrich analysis, the student could compare Mercator with an equal-area projection (e.g. Peters) to evaluate different balances of accuracy and usefulness.
The conclusion summarises arguments but does not synthesise the implications for how we generate and trust models as knowledge tools; a deeper reflection is needed.
The shift into natural sciences is abrupt and lacks a clear topic sentence linking climate models back to the prescribed title and knowledge question.
An open research question is engaging, but in TOK the knowledge question should be posed and explored within the body to guide sustained analysis.

The Buienradar and India forecast examples illustrate geographic variation but lack quantitative accuracy metrics (e.g. mean error). Including such data would allow a more critical evaluation of usefulness.
The essay fails to discuss how inaccurate forecasts can mislead stakeholders; addressing this counterclaim would deepen the evaluation of utility versus error.
Quoting Matthew Edney adds authoritative support for the claim that all flat maps distort reality, reinforcing the connection between expert knowledge and the TOK discussion.
The citation of Edney lacks full bibliographic detail (publisher, year). Consistent and complete referencing is required for academic rigour.
In-text citations are missing for some web sources. All factual claims and quotations should be clearly attributed to corresponding entries in the reference list.
The final question feels tacked on rather than emerging organically from the analysis. It would be stronger if introduced earlier as the guiding knowledge question.
The references heading appears before the final reflections are fully developed. Ensuring a clear structural separation between conclusion and bibliography will improve coherence.
References are inconsistently formatted and lack access dates. Adopting a uniform citation style (MLA, APA or Chicago) will enhance the essayâs academic integrity.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
